Saul was just too busy seeking David. At times, as we go back to Psalm 11, and we look at verse number three, if the foundations be destroyed, what can the righteous do? At times, the circumstances in our society can have an adverse effect on us. We, during times of great difficulty, when it seems as if the foundations are being destroyed, we may be led to conclude that there's no hope. We may be led to think something like this, there's nothing we can do. We must resist the tendency to focus on the circumstances and to be adversely impacted by them. We need to stop living under the circumstances and being discouraged by all that is going on in our society. What we must do is not ask the question that ends verse number three, but follow what takes place in verse number four. We must shift our focus upward as we see David doing in verse number four. The reality is that when we begin to look at this verse of Scripture and begin to think about a society that's falling apart, we can get into the position of what's it worth anyway? What can we really do? Can we really make a difference? But you know what that's doing? That's keeping God out of the equation. That's just looking upon what you or what I would might be able to accomplish. But we find that the circumstances that are revealed in verse number three turns for us to verse number four. And if you're taking notes, I have just simply said number two, our confidence. We see the circumstances, but I want you to note the confidence of David in verse number four. And this is what's so vital for us and so important for us to understand. See, verse number three would really be a human perspective. It would be living on the temporal plane. There's nothing that can be accomplished. I mean, look at all that's going on, but verse number four is a godly perspective that's now looking at the eternal plane. Notice in verse number four, the Lord is in his holy temple. The Lord's throne is in heaven. His eyes behold, his eyelids try the children of men. Three quick thoughts from verse number four as we begin to think about shifting our focus upwards during those times of circumstances in which it seems to be overwhelming and discouraging that our confidence is in the fact that, number one, our God is on his throne. Our God is on his throne. When Saul was not fulfilling his duties as king of Israel, as David is being pursued, as he evaluates the circumstances in the society that he knows and he loves in his homeland, and he sees the very foundations eroding and being destroyed, the lawlessness and the wickedness, what David had to remember is that God is on his throne. For you and for me this evening, it's absolutely imperative that we're mindful of the fact that we're regularly reminding ourselves of the truth that our God is on his throne. The Bible declares very plainly in the book of Revelation, the Lord God omnipotent reigneth. He's never going to be defeated. He's never going to resign. He's on his throne and he rules and he reigns. And for that we ought to rejoice and it should give us confidence. Not only do we see from verse number four that our God is on his throne, but we find also in this verse of scripture that our God has all power. I'm thankful this evening, that though we are limitless, limited, excuse me, though we are limited and though we lack the power that we could accomplish certain things, we serve a God who has all power. We serve a God who is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think. And we begin to understand that when David shifts his focus here, the Lord is in his holy temple, the Lord's throne is in heaven, that David begins to have this perspective and understanding that our God is on the throne and our God has all power. And though David was being pursued and though these wicked things were taking place, he had to get his focus on God. And it's so important for us tonight that our focus be on the Lord because we can get distracted by so many things that are going on. We can get distracted by all of the wickedness and the lawlessness and the erosion of what we would think has been a society that was built on Judeo-Christian values, et cetera, et cetera. And the reality is that we understand that evil men and seducers shall wax worse and worse. I mean, God told us that, but yet we must not be discouraged. We must not be distracted. We must get our focus upon the fact that we serve the God who is on his throne and who has all power. I notice in verse number four, the Bible says, his eyes behold, his eyelids try the children of men. Not only is he on his throne and not only does he have all power, but we find that our God knows all things. He knows all things. He knows about it. And that ought to cause us to rest. It ought to cause us to rejoice because we find that David says, and the Lord put I my trust. Now, again, as we mentioned in Psalm nine in verse number 10, the Bible says, and they that know thy name will put their trust in thee. For thou Lord has not forsaken them that seek thee. And David knew who God was. He knew, verse number four, that the Lord is in his holy temple, that the Lord's throne is in heaven. He was aware of that. And he declares in verse number one that I've put my trust in the Lord. And we find that he, David, is recognizing that though he does not have all power, though he does not know all things, that he serves the God who does. And this evening, we begin to think about these verses of scripture and we see where the Bible says that his eyes behold in his eyelids, try the children of men. We find that God was working on the behalf of David as we illustrated last week, looking at those examples in the book of 1 Samuel where God was defending and God was working. Why? Because God sees and God knows. There are times in which we go through life and we might have this perspective no one really understands. But we fail to recognize that our God has a deep, intimate understanding of everything that we face and everything that we're dealing with. And our God will give us the strength and the grace that we need. And so really when we begin to look at this question that is asked at the end of verse number three, when we look at it from a human perspective, what can the righteous do? We can come to that place of saying nothing. I mean, it's just useless, it's hopeless. I mean, there's nothing that's gonna change this. It's going so quickly in the wrong direction and it's just pointless. And from a human perspective, we'll just kind of get out of the game, so to speak. Get on the sidelines, throw up our hands and say, what's the use? But when we have a proper perspective, when we see God in His rightful place, when we understand that He's on His throne, that He has all power, that He knows all things and He sees all things, then we understand that what the righteous can do is everything that God has commanded us to do. That we continue to go forth, we continue to do what God has called us to do, what He's commissioned us to do. Why? Because God will work and God will bless. And God sees and God works. And it's not based on my power or on your power, it's based on His power. He said this, but you shall receive power after that the Holy Ghost has come upon you. That means that I don't have the power, it's the Holy Spirit of God who empowers me. Amen? My words are not powerful and neither are yours. But God's words are powerful, are they not? It's quick and sharp, it's powerful, it's a living book. We find that as we go forth in the path in which God has laid before us to do the work that He has called us to do, we do not get our eyes on the fact that the foundations are eroding and look at the societal collapse. No, we keep our eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, and we understand that He is in control and He rules and He reigns and we just continue doing what He's called us to do. And then we leave all the results for God. It's a wonderful thing to be able to leave it in the hands of God, that God can accomplish and God can do what we cannot do, what we're incapable of doing. Have you ever tried to use a witty argument with someone to try to convince them of some Bible truth? I mean, you try to come up with the best illustrations and analogies and all this sort of thing and you know what you find out? Your words are not powerful. It's God's words that are powerful. We just give the truth. We just continue to trust God. The reality is that there are times in which as David is, and again as you study the book of Psalms, as you study and read through the Psalms, you find so often the transparency of the Psalmist, that David is pouring his heart out before the Lord. He's not the author of all the Psalms obviously, but in the ones that are the Davidic Psalms, we find that there's such transparency. And for us this evening, it helps us. Why? Because there are times that we face circumstances that we feel the same way. What's the point? No one wants to hear. No one cares. Those thoughts will go in your head, and it will discourage you, and it will defeat you. But no, we get our eyes on Jesus and say, He's on His throne. He's got all power. He knows all things. And by the way, He didn't tell me to go and to give the truth and to preach the gospel. He didn't tell me to do all of that so that I could have results. He told me to do it as a matter of obedience, as a matter of obedience. And as I obey Him, guess what He does? He blesses obedience. That principle is found throughout the Scriptures. And so we begin to look at this passage and begin to find in the life of David that there's so many relevant aspects to what he was dealing with and what he was facing. And yet he was not going to be shaken by it. Why? Because of the first statement in the Psalm, in the Lord put I my trust. And it's so vital for us this evening to have had that declared position that I've put my trust in the Lord. But look at all these things that are going on. Yeah, but I'm trusting God. I'm not trusting the government. I'm not trusting the, you fill in the blank, whatever it is that could be trusted. They will fail you, but God never will fail us. And it's a simple shift of perspective from the foundations being destroyed to the Lord being in His holy temple and the Lord's throne is in heaven and how we need to get our eyes on Jesus. Remember what the Psalmist said in Psalm 121, and I'll finish with this, I will lift up mine eyes unto the hills from whence cometh my help. That's an action, is it not? It's an action. I will lift up mine eyes. Something that we have to do, something that we have to purpose to do. Why? Because you know what your tendency is and my tendency is? It's to look at all of the temporal things because it's what's right in front of us. It's what we're seeing. It's what we're experiencing. It's what we're dealing with every day. And the Psalmist says, I will lift up mine eyes into the hills from whence cometh my help. My help cometh from the Lord, which made heaven and earth. And what a joy to know that our God is the creator and He is our helper. Let's pray together. Father, we love you. Thank you for accepting us. Thank you for the opportunity that you've given us to gather. And Lord, we're excited about the opportunity to just serve you in these days and to see what you could accomplish through us. I pray, God, that you would help us to be yielded and surrendered. Help us, Lord, to keep our eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith. We thank you for the prayer time that we can have on Wednesday evenings. And I pray, God, that you would bless and work through the corporate prayer time, Lord, that we know, we believe tonight that you hear and you answer prayer. And we're so grateful for that biblical truth. And I pray that you would just have your will and way throughout the remainder of the service tonight. For it's in Christ's name we pray. Amen.
